food_name: Char Siu Rice — Economy Rice Plate

local_names.romanized_local: Chā shāo fàn / Cai png / Chap peng

aliases: 叉烧饭 / 杂饭 (Chā shāo fàn / Zá fàn)

chinese: 叉烧饭 / 杂饭 (Chā shāo fàn / Zá fàn)

food_subgroup: Economy rice plate: steamed white rice + char siu (BBQ pork) + stir-fried vegetable side. High carbohydrate (~78g per plate), high sodium (~900mg per plate).

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Economy Rice as a prepared Singapore hawker set meal. Closest parent would be a rice-based mixed dish with pork and vegetables; no exact FoodOn term available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice set meal with pork and vegetables (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Singapore economy rice hawker plate)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KDA used as closest parent for rice-based set meal with pork and vegetables. No exact FoodEx2 code for Singapore cai png / economy rice format.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Singapore economy rice hawker plate as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore hawker economy rice plate comprising approximately 200g steamed white rice, 80g char siu (BBQ pork), and 80g stir-fried vegetable (cabbage or kai lan) with a light soy drizzle, totalling approximately 380g and ~605 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siu Rice Economy Plate — HPB Singapore Foodo database + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (char siu and vegetable components)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values derived from composite calculation: 200g steamed white rice (HPB Singapore Foodo), 80g char siu BBQ pork (USDA FDC SR Legacy + HPB), 80g stir-fried kai lan/cabbage (HPB Singapore Foodo), light soy drizzle (~20g). Total plate ~380g. Values weighted and normalised to per-100g basis. Confidence HIGH per input record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed white rice (Oryza sativa) as dominant GI driver; char siu glaze sugar and vegetable starch as minor contributors
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 65 assigned based on steamed white rice GI (University of Sydney GI Database: GI ~64–72 for white rice, medium category). Economy rice plate is dominated by steamed white rice (~200g of 380g total). Char siu glaze sugar and vegetable starch are minor contributors. GL per plate = (GI 65 × 75g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 48.75 per input record; alternative calculation using 78g total carbohydrate gives GL 50.7 — both values indicate VERY HIGH glycemic load. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium: ~900mg per plate (236.8mg per 100g × 380g), representing ~45% of the WHO 2g/day sodium limit in a single meal; char siu is the dominant sodium source (~600mg from 80g portion), with additional sodium from soy drizzle (~200mg) and vegetable oyster sauce (~100mg).
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request no soy drizzle (saves ~200mg sodium), consider substituting char siu with a lower-sodium protein such as steamed chicken, and limit economy rice plate frequency to no more than 3 times per week; two vegetable sides instead of one protein side is a practical sodium-reduction strategy at the hawker stall.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load: GL ~49 per plate (GI 65, 78g total carbohydrate per plate including 66g from rice and ~10g from char siu glaze sugar); both rice starch and char siu glaze sugar must be counted for postprandial glucose management in T2DM.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request half-rice (reduces GL to ~25–30), pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow gastric emptying, avoid the soy drizzle to reduce added sugar, and monitor postprandial glucose as economy rice portions at hawker stalls frequently exceed the standard 200g rice serving used in this record.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.5g per 100g; ~5.7g per plate from char siu BBQ pork) combined with high sodium (~900mg per plate) and moderate cholesterol (~68mg per plate); char siu is prepared with honey, hoisin, and soy marinade which contributes both sodium and added sugar.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit char siu rice to occasional consumption, request a smaller char siu portion (40g instead of 80g) to halve saturated fat and sodium from the protein component, increase the vegetable side to two portions, and choose steamed or boiled protein alternatives when available at the economy rice stall.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (~900mg per plate) and moderate saturated fat from char siu (~5.7g per plate) may contribute to systemic inflammation; vegetable side (kai lan) provides Vitamin C (~15mg) and folate which are beneficial for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: An acceptable meal for RA patients with portion modification: request half-rice, extra vegetable side, and skip the soy drizzle to reduce sodium and inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siu Rice Economy Plate (叉烧饭 / 杂饭, cai png) is the most ubiquitous daily meal format at Singapore hawker centres. A standard plate comprises approximately 200g steamed white jasmine rice, 80g Cantonese-style BBQ pork (char siu — roasted with honey, hoisin sauce, five-spice, soy, and sugar glaze), and 80g stir-fried vegetable (typically kai lan or cabbage cooked with oyster sauce and garlic), finished with a light soy sauce drizzle, totalling approximately 380g per plate. Nutritional profile per plate: ~605 kcal, 20g protein, 16g fat (5.7g saturated), 78g carbohydrate (66g from rice starch + ~10g from char siu glaze sugar — both must be counted for T2DM management), ~900mg sodium. GI ~65 (medium, steamed white rice dominant), GL ~49 per plate — VERY HIGH for a single meal. The high GL makes this a significant postprandial glucose challenge for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; requesting half-rice reduces GL to approximately 25–30. Sodium is high at ~900mg per plate (45% of WHO daily limit), primarily from char siu marinade and soy drizzle, making this a concern for hypertensive patients. NOT halal (contains pork). Dairy-free. Economy rice (cai png / chap peng / 杂饭) is a self-service format where diners select protein and vegetable sides over rice; char siu is the most popular protein choice. Sources: HPB Singapore Foodo database + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: how many calories in economy rice
char siu rice GI glycemic index
is economy rice good for diabetes
meal_context: Singapore Chinese hawker breakfast or lunch

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values derived from composite calculation anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary regional source for Singapore hawker dishes) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (char siu pork component). Input record confidence is 1.0 (HIGH). GI value of 65 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for steamed white rice (GI 64–72), which dominates the plate composition. All core macros are complete and internally consistent. Plate-level totals (605 kcal, 20g protein, 16g fat, 78g carbohydrate, 900mg sodium) are consistent with per-100g values scaled to 380g serving.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singapore hawker economy rice plate is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry; values would require individual component fatty acid profiling not available in approved sources as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite prepared dish is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FDC for this specific combination. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FDC for steamed rice, char siu pork, and stir-fried vegetables — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). All core macros (protein_g 5.3, fat_g 4.2, carbohydrate_g 20.5) are non-zero and fully supported. GL per plate calculated as (65 × 75g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 48.75 per input record; alternative calculation using 78g total carbohydrate gives GL 50.7 — both values indicate VERY HIGH glycemic load. NOT halal (contains pork char siu). Economy rice portions at hawker stalls frequently exceed the standard 200g rice serving; actual GL may be higher in practice. Half-rice option reduces GL to approximately 25–30.
